content.text: Self-hosted deployment means running your AI agent stack entirely on infrastructure you own or lease — physical servers in a data center, VMs in a private cloud, or bare-metal machines in your own facility. No compute, storage, or networking is delegated to a public cloud provider. You install the operating system, container runtime, orchestrator, databases, monitoring, and secrets management yourself and take full responsibility for their operation.

does not mean "uncontainerized": most mature self-hosted deployments use Kubernetes or Docker Swarm to run agent workloads as containers, but those orchestrators run on hardware you control rather than on a managed cloud service.

The model ranges from a single GPU workstation running Ollama and a FastAPI agent server, all the way to a multi-rack Kubernetes cluster hosting dozens of agent microservices, a self-hosted vector database, Prometheus/Grafana observability, and HashiCorp Vault for secrets — entirely air-gapped from the public internet.

For many organizations the decision is not "cloud vs. self-hosted" out of philosophical preference — it is driven by hard constraints: regulated industries (defense, healthcare, finance, government) that prohibit sending data to external APIs; enterprises with existing data-center investments they must justify; or teams that need to run open-weight models locally without paying per-token costs.
patient records, legal documents, classified data, and financial transactions never leave your network perimeter — no third-party subprocessor risk and no GDPR cross-border transfer concerns.
you can run open-weight models (Llama 3, Mistral, Qwen, Gemma) that cannot be accessed via a managed API, fine-tuned models specific to your domain, or even proprietary in-house models.
a large inference cluster running millions of tokens per day may be cheaper to own than to pay API providers at per-token rates, especially if hardware is amortized over multiple years.
some government and healthcare frameworks (FedRAMP High, ITAR, CJIS) require on-premises or private-cloud deployment where you can demonstrate physical control over hardware.
every byte stays within your network. You choose what goes where — no vendor logging, no training on your data, no API terms-of-service surprises.
your stack is built on open standards (Docker, Kubernetes, PostgreSQL, Redis). Switching LLM backends means changing one config line, not migrating cloud accounts.

internal GPU inference eliminates the network round-trip to a remote API. Local Llama 3 on an A100 returns tokens in milliseconds; external APIs add 50–500 ms of network latency before the first token.
fully disconnected deployments are possible — no internet required. Suitable for classified environments, factory floors, ships at sea, and other disconnected scenarios.
you can tune GPU memory allocation, quantization (INT4, INT8, FP16), batch sizes, and inference parameters for your exact model and workload without constraints from a managed service.
for very high inference volumes, owned GPU hardware can be 3–10x cheaper than API pricing once amortized. A single A100 80 GB card handles millions of tokens per day.

you own every failure. OS patches, Kubernetes upgrades, certificate renewals, disk replacements, network changes — all land on your team. A cloud provider handles this transparently; self-hosting does not.
GPU servers capable of running 70B parameter models cost $30,000–$300,000 per node. The economics only work at sustained, predictable load; burst capacity is hard to provision cheaply.
you cannot add 100 GPUs for a weekend batch job and release them Monday. Capacity is fixed at what you purchase. Underprovisioned means queuing; overprovisioned means idle hardware.

frontier closed models (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet) are not available for local hosting. Self-hosted open-weight models are typically one generation behind the frontier, which matters for complex reasoning tasks.
network segmentation, firewall rules, CVE patching, and intrusion detection are entirely on you. A misconfigured firewall or unpatched kernel is your problem, not a vendor's.
operating a self-hosted agent platform requires DevOps/SRE expertise in Kubernetes, networking, storage, and GPU management — roles that are expensive to hire and retain.

The fundamental pattern mirrors cloud deployment but with every managed service replaced by a self-operated equivalent. Agent code runs as containers; a task queue decouples task submission from execution; a state store holds results; a secrets manager holds credentials; and an observability stack collects metrics, logs, and traces. The key difference is that
you provision, maintain, and repair every layer
A typical self-hosted agent request lifecycle:
An internal client (web app, CLI, another service) sends an HTTPS request to your load balancer (Nginx or HAProxy) over the private network or VPN.
The load balancer routes to the API server pod (FastAPI), which authenticates the request, enqueues a task message on Redis or RabbitMQ, and returns
Agent worker pods (Celery workers or custom poll loops) consume tasks from the queue. Each worker fetches its API key from HashiCorp Vault (or reads a mounted Kubernetes Secret) at startup.
The agent calls the local LLM inference server (Ollama on

) or — if the task warrants frontier capability — routes to an external LLM API over an egress-allowed connection. It calls tools, stores intermediate state in PostgreSQL, and writes the final result.
or receives a webhook callback. Prometheus scrapes all pods on
; Grafana dashboards display real-time latency, queue depth, and GPU utilization.

attrs.alt: Self-hosted agent deployment: internal clients connect over VPN to an Nginx load balancer, routing to containerized agent workers and API server managed by Kubernetes. Workers call a local GPU LLM server or optionally an external API. Redis handles task queuing, PostgreSQL stores state, Qdrant serves vectors, and Prometheus/Grafana provide observability — all within a private data center boundary.

content.text: Self-hosting is the right choice when one or more of the following conditions apply:
regulated data (PHI, PII, classified) must not leave your network. An external API call is categorically forbidden regardless of the provider's certifications.
the deployment environment has no internet connectivity — factory floors, submarines, classified facilities, industrial control systems.
millions of tokens per day at a price point where owned hardware is cheaper than API billing, and load is sustained enough to keep GPUs utilized.
your agent relies on a proprietary fine-tuned model or an open-weight base model that is not available through any managed API.
your organization already operates on-premises infrastructure with spare capacity; the marginal cost of adding agent workloads is low.
Do not default to self-hosting for perceived cost savings alone. Unless inference volume is very high and load is predictable, the staff cost of operations usually exceeds the API cost difference.

choose CPU-only servers for agents that call external LLM APIs; add GPU nodes (A100, RTX 4090, H100) if you will run local inference. Size RAM for model weights plus KV cache: a 70B model in FP16 requires ~140 GB VRAM, so two A100 80 GB cards with NVLink are the minimum.
Install the container runtime and orchestrator:

install Docker Engine and either Kubernetes (k3s for small clusters, kubeadm or Rancher for large ones) or Docker Swarm. For GPU nodes, install the NVIDIA Container Toolkit so containers can access GPU devices.
Set up a private container registry:
deploy Harbor or a simple Docker Registry container. Push your agent images here instead of Docker Hub so images never leave the network.

run Ollama as a Kubernetes Deployment with a GPU node selector, or deploy vLLM for production-grade serving with batching. Expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint so agent code can switch between local and external models by changing a base URL.

install Redis (task queue), PostgreSQL (state), Qdrant (vectors), and HashiCorp Vault (secrets) as Kubernetes StatefulSets with persistent volumes. Use Helm charts for each; they handle init containers, config maps, and readiness probes.

store all credentials in Vault. Use the Vault Agent Injector sidecar or the External Secrets Operator to mount secrets as files or environment variables into agent pods without storing them in Kubernetes Secrets in plain text.

deploy Nginx Ingress Controller and cert-manager. Use an internal CA to issue TLS certificates for all services so traffic is encrypted even inside the cluster. Configure NetworkPolicies to enforce that agent pods can only reach the queue, state store, and LLM service — not arbitrary internal IPs.

write Kubernetes Deployments for agent workers and the API server. Configure Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) based on a custom metric: queue depth exported to Prometheus via a Redis exporter and surfaced as an external metric through kube-metrics-adapter.
install the kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart (Prometheus + Grafana + Alertmanager). Add Loki for log aggregation and Jaeger for distributed tracing. Instrument agent code with the OpenTelemetry SDK.

if the environment has no internet access, deploy a self-hosted Gitea instance and Gitea Actions runners. The pipeline builds images, pushes to your private registry, and applies updated Kubernetes manifests via kubectl or ArgoCD.
Agent worker calling a local Ollama server

"""agent_worker.py — agent that calls a self-hosted Ollama LLM server.

Ollama exposes an OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint.
Switch to an external API by changing OLLAMA_BASE_URL and the model name.
"""

import os
import json
import redis
from openai import OpenAI  # works with Ollama's OpenAI-compatible endpoint

# Ollama runs on the GPU node; address is an internal Kubernetes service DNS name.
OLLAMA_BASE_URL = os.environ.get("OLLAMA_BASE_URL", "http://ollama-service:11434/v1")
MODEL = os.environ.get("LLM_MODEL", "llama3:70b-instruct")

client = OpenAI(base_url=OLLAMA_BASE_URL, api_key="not-required")  # Ollama ignores the key

redis_client = redis.Redis(
    host=os.environ["REDIS_HOST"],
    port=int(os.environ.get("REDIS_PORT", 6379)),
    password=os.environ.get("REDIS_PASSWORD"),
    decode_responses=True,
)

def run_agent(task: dict) -> str:
    """Execute a single agent task using the local LLM."""
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant. Think step by step."},
        {"role": "user", "content": task["prompt"]},
    ]
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=MODEL,
        messages=messages,
        temperature=0.2,
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

def poll_queue():
    """Poll Redis queue, process tasks, store results."""
    queue_key = "agent:tasks"
    result_prefix = "agent:result:"

    while True:
        # BLPOP blocks until a task appears; timeout=0 means wait forever
        item = redis_client.blpop(queue_key, timeout=30)
        if item is None:
            continue  # timed out, loop again

        _, raw = item
        task = json.loads(raw)
        task_id = task["task_id"]

        try:
            result = run_agent(task)
            redis_client.setex(f"{result_prefix}{task_id}", 3600, json.dumps({"status": "done", "result": result}))
        except Exception as exc:
            redis_client.setex(f"{result_prefix}{task_id}", 3600, json.dumps({"status": "error", "error": str(exc)}))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    poll_queue()
Kubernetes Deployment for the agent worker with GPU node selector

# k8s/agent-worker-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: agent-worker
  namespace: agents
spec:
  replicas: 3  # initial replica count; HPA scales from here
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: agent-worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: agent-worker
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port: "8000"
        prometheus.io/path: "/metrics"
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: agent-worker-sa
      containers:
        - name: worker
          image: registry.internal.example.com/agents/worker:abc1234  # private registry, pinned SHA
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000  # Prometheus metrics endpoint
          env:
            - name: OLLAMA_BASE_URL
              value: "http://ollama-service.gpu-ns.svc.cluster.local:11434/v1"
            - name: LLM_MODEL
              value: "llama3:70b-instruct"
            - name: REDIS_HOST
              value: "redis-master.agents.svc.cluster.local"
            - name: REDIS_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: redis-credentials
                  key: password
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "512Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
            limits:
              memory: "2Gi"
              cpu: "2"
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 30
---
# Horizontal Pod Autoscaler based on Redis queue depth
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: agent-worker-hpa
  namespace: agents
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: agent-worker
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
    - type: External
      external:
        metric:
          name: redis_queue_depth  # custom metric from Prometheus adapter
          selector:
            matchLabels:
              queue: agent-tasks
        target:
          type: AverageValue
          averageValue: "5"  # scale up when average tasks per pod exceeds 5
Deploying Ollama with GPU support on Kubernetes

# k8s/ollama-deployment.yaml
# Requires: NVIDIA Container Toolkit installed on GPU nodes,
# nvidia-device-plugin DaemonSet running in the cluster.
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ollama
  namespace: gpu-ns
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ollama
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ollama
    spec:
      nodeSelector:
        accelerator: nvidia-gpu  # schedule only on GPU nodes
      containers:
        - name: ollama
          image: ollama/ollama:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 11434
          volumeMounts:
            - name: ollama-models
              mountPath: /root/.ollama  # model weights stored on persistent volume
          resources:
            limits:
              nvidia.com/gpu: "2"  # request 2 GPUs for a 70B model
      volumes:
        - name: ollama-models
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: ollama-models-pvc
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: ollama-service
  namespace: gpu-ns
spec:
  selector:
    app: ollama
  ports:
    - port: 11434
      targetPort: 11434
  clusterIP: None  # headless; agents call by DNS name, no load balancing needed

a hospital network needs a clinical documentation agent that reads physician notes, suggests ICD-10 diagnostic codes, and flags missing required fields. Patient data is PHI under HIPAA and cannot be sent to any external API. The team deploys a two-node Kubernetes cluster: one CPU node running the agent workers, API server, PostgreSQL, and Redis; one GPU node (two A100 80 GB cards) running Ollama with a fine-tuned Llama 3 70B model trained on medical coding examples. Agent workers call the GPU node's Ollama endpoint over the cluster-internal network. HashiCorp Vault issues short-lived database credentials to agent pods. Prometheus tracks coding accuracy metrics emitted by the agent; Grafana dashboards alert the clinical informatics team when the model's confidence drops below a threshold — signaling the need for human review.

"""clinical_coding_agent.py — ICD-10 code suggestion on self-hosted infrastructure.

All data stays on-premises. No network call leaves the private cluster.
"""

import os
import json
from openai import OpenAI
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, start_http_server
import time

# Metrics exposed on :8000/metrics for Prometheus to scrape
codes_suggested = Counter("agent_codes_suggested_total", "ICD-10 codes suggested")
coding_latency = Histogram("agent_coding_latency_seconds", "Time to complete one coding task")
low_confidence_cases = Counter("agent_low_confidence_total", "Cases where confidence < threshold")

client = OpenAI(
    base_url=os.environ["OLLAMA_BASE_URL"],  # http://ollama-service.gpu-ns.svc:11434/v1
    api_key="not-required",
)

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """
You are a clinical coding assistant trained on ICD-10-CM guidelines.
Given a physician's note, return a JSON object with:
  - codes: list of {code, description, confidence (0.0-1.0)}
  - missing_fields: list of documentation gaps that prevent accurate coding
Return only valid JSON. Do not add commentary outside the JSON.
"""

def suggest_codes(physician_note: str) -> dict:
    """Call the local fine-tuned model and parse the response."""
    start = time.time()
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="medcoder-llama3-70b",  # custom fine-tuned model pulled into Ollama
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
            {"role": "user", "content": physician_note},
        ],
        temperature=0.1,  # low temperature for deterministic coding
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
    )
    coding_latency.observe(time.time() - start)
    result = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)

    codes_suggested.inc(len(result.get("codes", [])))

    # Alert on low-confidence codes
    for code in result.get("codes", []):
        if code.get("confidence", 1.0) < 0.7:
            low_confidence_cases.inc()

    return result

if __name__ == "__main__":
    start_http_server(8000)  # Prometheus metrics
    # In production this would poll a Redis queue; simplified here for clarity
    sample_note = (
        "Patient presents with Type 2 diabetes mellitus, poorly controlled. "
        "Also reports intermittent chest pain on exertion over the past 2 weeks."
    )
    result = suggest_codes(sample_note)
    print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
Storing secrets in Kubernetes Secrets without encryption at rest:
Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded, not encrypted. By default, anyone with etcd access can decode them. Enable etcd encryption at rest (
) or use an external secrets manager like Vault.
No GPU memory isolation between pods:

by default, NVIDIA GPU memory is not isolated between containers sharing a device. Use MIG (Multi-Instance GPU) partitioning on A100/H100 cards to give each pod a dedicated GPU slice, or schedule exactly one LLM process per GPU.
means a pod restart can pull a different image than currently running, breaking reproducibility. Pin every image to a SHA digest in production manifests.

without NetworkPolicies, every pod can reach every other pod. A compromised agent container can reach the database, the secrets store, or any other service. Apply deny-all defaults and explicitly allow only the required pod-to-pod connections.

running a 70B model in FP32 requires 280 GB of VRAM — far more than available. Use 4-bit quantization (GGUF Q4_K_M in Ollama, or AWQ/GPTQ in vLLM) to fit a 70B model in two A100 80 GB cards. Accuracy loss is small for instruction-following tasks.

model weights on a PVC, PostgreSQL data, and Qdrant indexes must be backed up. Use Velero for Kubernetes-aware PVC snapshots. A disk failure without a backup means re-downloading or re-generating all data.
all Kubernetes manifests, Helm values, Vault policies, and Nginx configs belong in a Git repository. No manual kubectl apply in production — all changes go through CI/CD with review and audit trail.
write agent code against the OpenAI SDK with a configurable
. Point it at Ollama or vLLM locally, and at the Anthropic or OpenAI API in fallback scenarios. This decouples model choice from agent logic.
Apply resource requests and limits to every container:
without limits, one runaway agent pod can exhaust node memory and evict other workloads. Requests enable the scheduler to pack pods correctly; limits protect neighbors.
Pre-pull model weights during node bootstrap:

downloading a 40 GB model at pod startup creates a long initialization delay. Use a Kubernetes init container or a DaemonSet to pre-pull weights to the node's persistent volume during cluster setup, not at agent startup time.

store different model versions as separate Ollama tags or vLLM model names. Deploy new model versions alongside old ones and use feature flags or request routing to canary-test them before cutting over all traffic.

self-hosted means on-call engineers must know how to restart Vault, recover Qdrant from a snapshot, or extend a full PostgreSQL disk at 2 AM. Document each procedure and test it in a staging environment before you need it in production.

Self-hosted inference performance depends primarily on GPU hardware and quantization choice. On an A100 80 GB with a 4-bit quantized Llama 3 70B model served by vLLM with continuous batching, you can expect roughly 800–1,500 output tokens per second with 16 concurrent requests. That supports around 100–200 completed agent tasks per minute (assuming 500 output tokens per task) from a single GPU pair — competitive with many API tiers.

vLLM's PagedAttention and continuous batching serve multiple concurrent requests on a single GPU without the padding overhead of static batching. Use vLLM over raw Ollama for any production workload above a handful of concurrent users.
vLLM allocates a fixed fraction of GPU memory for the KV cache. For long-context agents (128k token context), set
to maximize cache space. Smaller cache means more cache evictions and re-computation, reducing throughput.
for models that do not fit on a single GPU, vLLM supports tensor parallelism across multiple GPUs on the same node via NVLink. Launch with
to split a 70B model across two A100s.

scale inference horizontally by adding GPU nodes and pointing a simple round-robin load balancer (Nginx upstream) at multiple vLLM instances. Each node runs one vLLM process; the load balancer distributes requests across them.

vLLM supports speculative decoding with a small draft model to accelerate the generation of predictable tokens. For agents that generate structured JSON or follow fixed templates, speculative decoding can increase throughput by 1.5–2x with no accuracy loss.
Serve open-weight models on local GPUs
Async task dispatch and worker coordination
Build, test, and deploy pipelines without internet
Private image hosting with vulnerability scanning
Never (unless you route to external API)

Self-hosted deployment concentrates security responsibility on your team. The surface area is large: node OS, Kubernetes control plane, container runtimes, network plugins, and every self-operated service. The following controls are essential:
to encrypt Secrets and ConfigMaps in etcd using AES-GCM or KMS envelope encryption backed by Vault.
deny all ingress and egress by default. Allow only the specific pod-to-pod connections the agent stack requires. Use a CNI plugin that enforces NetworkPolicy (Calico, Cilium, Antrea).
in all pod security contexts. Drop all Linux capabilities except those explicitly required.

kernel CVEs, containerd vulnerabilities, and GPU driver flaws must be patched promptly. Use a rolling node replacement strategy (cordon, drain, replace) so the cluster stays available during OS upgrades.

set a PodDisruptionBudget requiring at least one agent worker pod to remain available during voluntary disruptions (node drains, rolling upgrades). This prevents all workers from being evicted simultaneously.
integrate Trivy or Grype into the CI pipeline to scan agent images for CVEs before they are pushed to the private registry. Block deployments of images with critical-severity vulnerabilities.

run three control-plane nodes in an HA etcd quorum. A single control-plane node is a single point of failure: if it goes down, existing workloads continue running but no new pods can be scheduled and kubectl stops working.
Self-hosted deployment gives complete data sovereignty and model freedom, making it the only viable choice for air-gapped, classified, or strictly regulated agent workloads.
The architecture mirrors cloud deployment — stateless containers, external queues, external state stores — but every managed service is replaced by a self-operated equivalent.
Local LLM inference (Ollama, vLLM) eliminates per-token API costs and network latency, but requires GPU hardware and adds model management complexity.
Write agent code against an OpenAI-compatible interface with a configurable base URL so you can switch between local and external LLMs without changing agent logic.

The biggest risk is operational: patch cycles, backup failures, GPU driver issues, and Kubernetes upgrades all fall on your team. Invest in runbooks, on-call rotation, and staging environments before production.
Key security controls are NetworkPolicy enforcement, etcd encryption at rest, non-root containers, Vault-backed secrets, and regular node patching — skipping any of them creates material risk.

Self-hosting does not scale elastically — GPU capacity is fixed at what you buy. For bursty workloads, a cloud platform or hybrid approach (self-hosted for baseline, cloud for burst) is usually more practical.
